summary: CRISP Shared Services, Inc. generally supports ONC/ASTP's deregulatory approach focusing on value and outcomes rather than technical criteria, while emphasizing enforcement of Information Blocking rules. They support clarifications on automated access/use of EHI but oppose removal of the 'third party seeking modification use' exception and caution against the proposed definition of 'contract of adhesion' which could disrupt existing interoperability agreements.
specific_provisions: third party seeking modification use exception
key_arguments: Overregulation stifles innovation and shifts focus from patient care to compliance.
Automated technologies accessing EHI must comply with existing privacy and security standards.
The 'third party seeking modification use' exception is necessary to ensure data accuracy and should not be removed without clear guidance.
The proposed broad definition of 'contract of adhesion' threatens foundational interoperability agreements and could be presumed as information blocking.
recommendations: Retain the 'third party seeking modification use' exception or provide explicit guidance on applying Security and Preventing Harm exceptions if removed.
Maintain the Manner Exception Exhausted condition with proposed clarifications to prevent abuse.
If defining 'contract of adhesion,' adopt the common law definition rather than creating a new standard to preserve trust in interoperability agreements.
Continue monitoring AI tools to ensure alignment with FAVES principles.
notable_quote: CMS and ONC/ASTP should drive value rather than regulating standards and software and enforce its rules when those who should be sharing data do not.
